Caribou Coffee · Kiosk Pilot · Feb 2026
Hot Latte: POS vs Kiosk
POS vs Kiosk + Proposed
01 Size Selection Required Kiosk Win: +4pp XL Customer Decision: Default or No Default?
Kiosk — Current: Medium Pre-selected Absolute prices · Medium highlighted on load
Small — $5.74~19%
Medium — $6.08 (pre-selected)~50%
Large — $6.37~22%
Extra Large — $7.478.5% ↑ vs POS
Customer Questions
To default or not to default?
A default removes one tap for guests happy with Medium, but may anchor the size distribution — keeping Large and XL as "opt-up" decisions rather than equal choices.
Is Medium the right default?
Medium maps to POS's most common order (46.1%) and is the price shown in the menu header — natural anchor. But if kiosk's goal is to surface upgrade behavior, a no-default state forces each size to compete on equal footing. XL already outperforms POS 2× under the current Medium default; removal could push further, or stall out on friction.
Internal Note — Platform Consideration
Delta pricing as a kiosk config option: When a size modifier group has a default, there is a potential UX benefit to showing prices as adjustments relative to that default (e.g., "Large +$0.29" / "XL +$1.39") rather than absolute prices. This removes the sticker-shock effect of displaying the full item price per size — particularly for XL — and mirrors how guests mentally experience upgrades. Could be surfaced as a kiosk-specific setting scoped to modifier groups that have a defined default. The screenshot below illustrates this concept; note that this approach requires size to be modeled as a price-adjustment modifier, which affects how size prints on kitchen tickets (nested under item rather than preceding it) — an ops consideration that would need Caribou sign-off.
Internal Note — Kiosk Display Bug / Quirk
All priced modifiers display "+" regardless of sign. Currently the kiosk appends "+" to all modifier prices, including negative adjustments — resulting in "Small + −$0.39" as seen in the delta pricing mockup above. This is technically correct math but reads awkwardly to guests. A display fix to show negative-price modifiers as "−$0.39" (no leading +) would clean up the size selector and any other groups where discounts or downgrades are modeled as negative-price options.
Summary
XL is already over-indexing vs POS under the current Medium default — suggesting kiosk guests are naturally inclined to upgrade when sizes are visually equal. The primary customer question is whether Medium is anchoring the distribution in a way that undersells Large, and whether removing the default would meaningfully shift the mix or simply add friction. Either path benefits from delta pricing framing once the display fix for negative-price modifiers is in place.
02 Milk Selection Required Kiosk Win: +12.6pp Alt Milk
POS Defaults to 2% unless guest requests otherwise
2% Milk (default)~65%
Oat Milk7.5%
Almond Milk2.5%
Coconut Milk0.8%
Alt milk total16.1%
Kiosk (Current) 7 equal-weight tiles — all options visible
2% Milk (default)~60%
Oat Milk~12%
Coconut Milk5.4%
Half & Half (+$1)8.0%
Alt milk total28.7% ↑
Why kiosk wins here
Milk is REQUIRED at position 2 — every guest sees the options and actively considers them, even if 2% Milk remains the default. Unlike POS where 2% is applied silently unless the guest volunteers a preference, the equal-weight tile layout gives every option equal visual prominence. Guests who want 2% accept the default without an extra tap; guests who want something else are no longer dependent on knowing to ask. This is the kiosk's clearest discovery win, and the model for how required groups with meaningful choices should be presented.
03 Espresso Type, Temperature & Froth Currently Required — 3 groups Proposed: Collapse to Optional Summary
POS — Implicit
Decaf requests22.3%
Extra Hot17.3%
Froth changes<2%
Required taps0
Scroll real estate0
Current Kiosk — Flat Required
Espresso deviation1.8%
Temperature deviation12.5%
Froth deviation7.1%
Active taps required0 (pre-selected)
Scroll real estate~2 full screens
Internal name visible"Froth (3rd Party\KIOSK)"
Proposed — Nested Optional
→
Defaults visible at a glanceEspresso / Temp / Froth
Change via pencil icon✓ Per group
Active taps to proceed0
Scroll real estate1 compact row
Mirrors POS behavior✓
Proposed Config Change
All three groups are pre-selected to their defaults — guests don't need to tap anything to proceed today. The cost is scroll real estate: ~2 full screens of tile grids that signal "decisions needed" even when ~90% of guests change nothing. Nesting into a single compact optional row mirrors POS's implicit behavior exactly. Nothing is hidden — defaults are readable and each group is a tap away via the pencil icon.
04 Flavor Shots Optional Gap: −14pp vs POS Proposed: Entry-Point Tile
POS — Entry Point Button
Flavor attach rate~20.2%
Full Vanilla44.0% of orders
Full Caramel19.4%
Entry point"$ Add Flavor" always visible
Scroll depth0
Current Kiosk — Inline Flat Grid
Flavor attach (est.)~25–30%
Vanilla + Caramel share78% of all selects
Options inline32 — flat undifferentiated grid
Position in flow8 of 11 — past 7 other groups
Proposed — Nested Entry Tile
Entry tile (collapsed) → tap → sub-screen
Flavor Shots
See options
✎
↓
Entry tileSingle row — "See options"
Full list in sub-screen✓ Cancel / Continue pattern
Post-selection tile state"Flavor Shots: Vanilla ✓"
Scroll real estate saved~3 full screens
Mirrors POS pattern✓ Entry-point → sub-screen
>
Same Pattern, Two Problems Solved
POS gets 20%+ flavor attach because "$ Add Flavor" is an always-visible, zero-scroll entry point. Guests who find it on kiosk choose Vanilla or Caramel 78% of the time — they just need surfacing, not more options. The nested entry tile is the kiosk equivalent of the POS flavor button, with one UX upgrade: the tile reflects the current selection after choosing ("Flavor Shots: Vanilla ✓"), giving guests confirmation that POS's static button never provides. The Cancel / Continue pattern in the sub-screen is already the platform convention — no new interaction model required.
05 Toppings & Cold Foam Optional Cold Foam: Kiosk 3.2% vs POS ~0%
POS — Toppings screen Requires barista to offer — rarely prompted
Cold Foam attach rate~0% (not prompted)
Whip attach rate~1.4%
Kiosk (Current) — Screens 8–9 Position 11 of 11 — discovered only by scrollers
Cold Foam attach rate3.2% ↑
Position11 of 11 — last group
OpportunityMove adjacent to Froth/Milk section
Discovery Win — despite position
3.2% cold foam attach vs nearly zero at POS — purely because POS baristas never surface it. If Espresso/Temp/Froth collapse into the Customize block, cold foam becomes a natural neighbor to Froth and can be elevated to where guests are already thinking about texture. Moving it earlier could push attach 2–3× without any structural change to the flow.
06 Sugar & Sweeteners · Mix-Ins Optional
POS — Mix-Ins screen Guest must verbally request
Honey attach rate5.8%
Sugar / sweeteners~7–14%
Kiosk (Current) — Screens 7–8 Positions 9–10 of 11
Honey attach rate6.2% (≈ POS)
Turbinado6.2%
Input patternStepper (+/−) vs tile-select elsewhere
Note
Sugar & Sweeteners uses +/− stepper inputs while most other groups use tile-select — an inconsistent mid-scroll interaction pattern. Honey kiosk attach (6.2%) is comparable to POS (5.8%), suggesting guests who reach this section convert well regardless.
